subject: Physics
title: Cherenkov Radiation
description: Learn Cherenkov Radiation in one minute.

hook.text: Nothing outruns light in a vacuum—but particles in water can beat light's local speed there.

buildup.text: Light slows down in water to about 75% of its vacuum speed. A fast enough particle can outpace it.

discovery.text: When a particle exceeds water's light speed, it produces an eerie blue glow called Cherenkov radiation.

twist.text: This blue glow is the optical equivalent of a sonic boom—a shockwave of light, not sound.

climax.text: Nuclear reactors underwater glow blue because of Cherenkov radiation from high-speed electrons.

punchline.text: Faster than light in water. A blue glow that breaks no rules.

quiz.question: What causes the blue glow in nuclear reactor pools?
options: Particles exceeding light's speed in water produce Cherenkov radiation
The water absorbs gamma rays and re-emits blue light
